retrieved_docs.title: Early Childhood Caries -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Chlorhexidine varnish can also be applied to protect the tooth surface. Likewise, bimonthly application of 10% povidone-iodine has been recommended by some authors for its antimicrobial effect against Streptococcus mutans.

retrieved_docs.title: Ventilator Safety -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Oral cavity hygiene should be maintained.  This may not always be possible because the endotracheal tube may not allow for meticulous cleaning at the back of the throat. However, the teeth should be brushed, and the mouth should regularly be rinsed. The use of chlorhexidine has been the standard of care for many years. Newer studies have shown no impact on the rate of ventilator-associated pneumonia, length of ICU stay, or time of mechanical ventilation. [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Early Childhood Caries -- Deterrence and Patient Education -- Children's Oral Hygiene
retrieved_docs.content: Healthcare providers should educate parents and caregivers about children's oral hygiene. Fewer Streptococcus mutans have been found in children who toothbrush with fluoridated toothpaste. [40] Therefore, parents should be advised to start toothbrushing their children's teeth with a soft brush and fluoridated toothpaste when the first tooth erupts. [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Halitosis -- Treatment / Management -- Mouth Rinses
retrieved_docs.content: For effective control of halitosis, antibacterial agents such as chlorhexidine, zinc, triclosan, and cetylpyridinium chloride are recommended for usage. These have different mechanisms of action. Although chlorhexidine is undoubtedly the best for inhibition of the production of VSCs, it may have an unpleasant side effect of the unsightly staining of teeth. [3] A combination of these agents, along with essential oils, is incorporated in several commercially available preparations. [45]

retrieved_docs.content: Dietary Fluoride Supplements

retrieved_docs.title: The Role of Fluoride on Caries Prevention -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Fluoride mouthwash helps prevent dental caries by increasing fluoride levels in dental plaque and saliva. [19] The mouthwash must be used at a different time of the day than when toothbrushing to increase its availability in the biofilm and optimize its topical effect. [28] The concentration depends on the frequency of use. Over-the-counter solutions of 230 ppm sodium fluoride are indicated for daily rinsing, and those containing 900 ppm for weekly use. [29] They are not recommended for children under 6 years due to the risk of swallowing, as the risk/benefit ratio favors risk. [6] Parents must supervise the use of fluoride mouth rinses in permanent dentition, as this has proven to be more efficacious than unsupervised utilization. Fluoride Varnish, Gels, and Foams

retrieved_docs.title: Angular Chelitis -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: Xylitol or chlorhexidine acetate/xylitol gum decreased AC in patients 60 years of age and older in a clinical trial by Simons et al. [16] Allergen avoidance is necessary for allergic contact dermatitis-induced AC Rinsing of the mouth after inhaled corticoid steroid administration in COPD/asthma patients decreases the risk of oral candidiasis Eradicate of staph and strep with mupirocin or bacitracin application to the anterior nares in colonized individuals who suffer from bacterial AC Identify reservoirs of infection, such as dentures, and ensure proper cleaning and possibly overnight storage in hypochlorite or chlorhexidine Early referral to a prosthodontist is imperative if dentures are a contributing risk factor In edentulous and immunosuppression patients, preventative therapy of barrier ointments (petrolatum, zinc oxide) or daily imidazole cream

retrieved_docs.title: Gingivitis -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: If it is a drug-induced gingival overgrowth, the physician can change the medication to improve the outcome of treatment of the condition. If it is due to a nutritional deficiency, supplements can be prescribed. Medications in the form of antiseptic mouthwash that contains chlorhexidine can also be prescribed in conjunction with the mechanical removal of plaque. It has been suggested that the use of chlorhexidine mouthwashes in addition to the usual toothbrushing and interproximal cleaning leads to a significant decrease in the build-up of dental biofilm. The concentration of the chlorhexidine rinse does not affect its effectiveness. [19]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 3 -- Community 1377
retrieved_docs.content: Chlorhexidin is linked to a wide range of medicated shampoos that incorporate chlorhexidine gluconate as a primary active moiety, often combined with additional antifungal or keratolytic agents. Specific formulations in which chlorhexidin has the active moiety include “chlorhexidine gluconate 10 MG/ML / ketoconazole 2.5 MG/ML Medicated Shampoo,” “chlorhexidine gluconate 20 MG/ML / ketoconazole 10 MG/ML / salicyloyl phytosphingosine 0.5 MG/ML Medicated Shampoo,” “chlorhexidine gluconate 20 MG/ML / ketoconazole 10 MG/ML Medicated Shampoo [Vital‑Ketodine],” “chlorhexidine gluconate 20 MG/ML / miconazole nitrate 20 MG/ML Medicated Shampoo [MiconaHex+Triz],” “chlorhexidine gluconate 30 MG/ML / climbazole 5 MG/ML / salicyloyl phytosphingosine 0.5 MG/ML Medicated Shampoo,” “chlorhexidine gluconate 40 MG/ML / salicyloyl phytosphingosine 0.5 MG/ML Medicated Shampoo,” and “chlorhexidine gluconate 40 MG/ML Medicated Shampoo [Vital‑Chlor].” In addition, chlorhexidin is identified as an ingredient in several broader shampoo products, namely “chlorhexidine / climbazole / salicyloyl phytosphingosine Medicated Shampoo,” “chlorhexidine / ketoconazole / salicyloyl phytosphingosine Medicated Shampoo,” “chlorhexidine / ketoconazole Medicated Shampoo,” “chlorhexidine / miconazole / salicyloyl phytosphingosine Medicated Shampoo,” “chlorhexidine / salicyloyl phytosphingosine Medicated Shampoo,” and the simple “chlorhexidine Medicated Shampoo.” Across all these products, chlorhexidine (often in the form of chlorhexidine gluconate) serves as a key antiseptic component, while the accompanying agents such as ketoconazole, miconazole nitrate, climbazole, and salicyloyl phytosphingosine provide antifungal or keratolytic effects, creating combination therapies aimed at treating scalp infections, dandruff, and related dermatologic conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 2 -- Community 1058
retrieved_docs.content: Chlorhexidine (chlorhexidin) gluconate is the core antiseptic ingredient in a wide range of medicated shampoos, formulated at concentrations from 10 mg/mL to 40 mg/mL. These shampoos are combined with antifungal agents—ketoconazole (10 mg/mL) in products such as chlorhexidine 20 mg/mL / ketoconazole 10 mg/mL (branded Keto‑C, Vital‑Ketodine) and chlorhexidine 23 mg/mL / ketoconazole 10 mg/mL (Ketochlor); miconazole nitrate (20 mg/mL) in chlorhexidine 20 mg/mL / miconazole nitrate 20 mg/mL (Malaseb, MiconaHex+Triz); and climbazole (5 mg/mL) in chlorhexidine 30 mg/mL / climbazole 5 mg/mL. Anti‑inflammatory hydrocortisone (10 mg/mL) is incorporated in triple‑combination formulas such as chlorhexidine 20 mg/mL / hydrocortisone 10 mg/mL / ketoconazole 10 mg/mL. The keratolytic and skin‑conditioning agent salicyloyl phytosphingosine (0.5 mg/mL) appears alone with chlorhexidine or in multi‑agent blends (e.g., chlorhexidine 20 mg/mL / ketoconazole 10 mg/mL / salicyloyl phytosphingosine 0.5 mg/mL; chlorhexidine 20 mg/mL / miconazole nitrate 20 mg/mL / salicyloyl phytosphingosine 0.5 mg/mL; chlorhexidine 30 mg/mL / climbazole 5 mg/mL / salicyloyl phytosphingosine 0.5 mg/mL; chlorhexidine 40 mg/mL / salicyloyl phytosphingosine 0.5 mg/mL). Additional antiseptic bases such as chloroxylenol (10 mg/mL) are used in formulations like chloroxylenol / miconazole / salicylic acid and chloroxylenol 10 mg/mL / miconazole nitrate 20 mg/mL / salicylic acid 20 mg/mL. Other dose forms within the medicated shampoo category include miconazole‑only shampoos, urea 100 mg/mL shampoo, and chlorhexidine 40 mg/mL shampoo (Vital‑Chlor). Collectively, these varied dose forms illustrate how medicated shampoos can be tailored to provide antimicrobial, antifungal, anti‑inflammatory, and keratolytic therapy for dermatologic and otic conditions, with specific brand names and concentration combinations defining each product’s therapeutic focus.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 2 -- Community 702
retrieved_docs.content: Medicated Shampoo is a therapeutic topical preparation offered in numerous dose‑form variants that are defined by specific active‑ingredient combinations and concentrations. The primary antiseptic moiety, chlorhexidine (often as chlorhexidine gluconate), appears in a wide range of formulations, including chlorhexidine 10 mg/mL, 20 mg/mL, 23 mg/mL, 30 mg/mL, and 40 mg/mL. Chlorhexidine is combined with antifungal agents such as ketoconazole (e.g., chlorhexidine 20 mg/mL / ketoconazole 10 mg/mL, marketed as Ketochlor), miconazole nitrate (e.g., chlorhexidine 20 mg/mL / miconazole nitrate 20 mg/mL, marketed as Malaseb or MiconaHex+Triz), and climbazole (e.g., chlorhexidine 30 mg/mL / climbazole 5 mg/mL). Anti‑inflammatory hydrocortisone is incorporated in multi‑agent products (e.g., chlorhexidine 20 mg/mL / hydrocortisone 10 mg/mL / ketoconazole 10 mg/mL). Keratolytic and barrier‑supporting agents such as salicyloyl phytosphingosine (0.5 mg/mL) are added to several chlorhexidine‑based shampoos (e.g., chlorhexidine 20 mg/mL / ketoconazole 10 mg/mL / salicyloyl phytosphingosine 0.5 mg/mL; chlorhexidine 30 mg/mL / climbazole 5 mg/mL / salicyloyl phytosphingosine 0.5 mg/mL; chlorhexidine 40 mg/mL / salicyloyl phytosphingosine 0.5 mg/mL). Stand‑alone chlorhexidine preparations (e.g., chlorhexidine 40 mg/mL, Vital‑Clor) are also listed. Additional medicated shampoos contain other active agents: miconazole alone or with salicylic acid (e.g., miconazole nitrate 20 mg/mL / salicylic acid 20 mg/mL), chloroxylenol 10 mg/mL combined with miconazole nitrate 20 mg/mL and salicylic acid 20 mg/mL, urea 100 mg/mL, and zinc pyrithione 3 mg/mL (the latter includes the inactive ingredient alpha‑tocopherol). These diverse formulations deliver antiseptic, antifungal, anti‑inflammatory, keratolytic, and moisturizing effects, targeting scalp infections, fungal dermatoses, inflammatory conditions, hyperkeratosis, and general dermatologic disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 2 -- Community 268
retrieved_docs.content: Chlorhexidin is identified as the active moiety in a wide range of chlorhexidine‑based medicated shampoes, encompassing formulations with varying concentrations of chlorhexidine gluconate and diverse additional agents. Specifically, chlorhexidin is the active component of chlorhexidine gluconate 10 mg/mL / ketoconazole 2.5 mg/mL Medicated Shampoo, chlorhexidine gluconate 10 mg/mL Medicated Shampoo, chlorhexidine gluconate 20 mg/mL / hydrocortisone 10 mg/mL / ketoconazole 10 mg/mL Medicated Shampoo, chlorhexidine gluconate 20 mg/mL / ketoconazole 10 mg/mL Medicated Shampoo (Keto‑C), chlorhexidine gluconate 20 mg/mL / miconazole nitrate 20 mg/mL Medicated Shampoo (Malaseb), chlorhexidine gluconate 20 mg/mL / miconazole nitrate 20 mg/mL Medicated Shampoo (MiconaHex+Triz), chlorhexidine gluconate 20 mg/mL Medicated Shampoo, chlorhexidine gluconate 23 mg/mL / ketoconazole 10 mg/mL Medicated Shampoo (Ketochlor), chlorhexidine gluconate 30 mg/mL / climbazole 5 mg/mL / salicyloyl phytosphingosine 0.5 mg/mL Medicated Shampoo, chlorhexidine gluconate 40 mg/mL / salicyloyl phytosphingosine 0.5 mg/mL Medicated Shampoo, chlorhexidine gluconate 40 mg/mL Medicated Shampoo, and chlorhexidine gluconate 40 mg/mL Medicated Shampoo (Vital‑Chlor). In addition to being the active moiety, chlorhexidin is listed as an ingredient in combination shampoos that pair chlorhexidine with other agents, including chlorhexidine / climbazole / salicyloyl phytosphingosine Medicated Shampoo, chlorhexidine / miconazole / salicyloyl phytosphingosine Medicated Shampoo, chlorhexidine / miconazole Medicated Shampoo, chlorhexidine / salicyloyl phytosphingosine Medicated Shampoo, and chlorhexidine Medicated Shampoo. Collectively, these relationships illustrate that chlorhexidin serves both as the principal active substance and as a component of multi‑ingredient formulations designed to provide antiseptic, antifungal, anti‑inflammatory, or keratolytic effects in scalp and skin care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 2 -- Community 362
retrieved_docs.content: Chlorhexidin (chlorhexidine gluconate) is the core antiseptic active moiety in a broad range of medicated shampoos that are formulated to treat scalp infections, fungal overgrowth, bacterial colonization, and scaling dermatologic conditions. The product class “Medicated Shampoo” includes numerous dose forms that combine chlorhexidine at concentrations of 10 MG/ML, 20 MG/ML, 30 MG/ML, or 40 MG/ML with antifungal agents such as ketoconazole (2.5 MG/ML or 10 MG/ML), miconazole nitrate (20 MG/ML), climbazole (5 MG/ML), or with keratolytic and soothing agents such as salicyloyl phytosphingosine (0.5 MG/ML) and salicylic acid (18 MG/ML). Specific branded formulations include Keto‑C and Vital‑Ketodine (chlorhexidine 20 MG/ML + ketoconazole 10 MG/ML), Malaseb and MiconaHex+Triz (chlorhexidine 20 MG/ML + miconazole nitrate 20 MG/ML), and Vital‑Chlor (chlorhexidine 40 MG/ML). Additional combinations listed are chlorhexidine 20 MG/ML + ketoconazole 10 MG/ML + salicyloyl phytosphingosine 0.5 MG/ML, chlorhexidine 30 MG/ML + climbazole 5 MG/ML + salicyloyl phytosphingosine 0.5 MG/ML, chlorhexidine 40 MG/ML + salicyloyl phytosphingosine 0.5 MG/ML, and chlorhexidine alone at 10 MG/ML, 20 MG/ML, or 40 MG/ML. The generic category also encompasses shampoos that pair chloroxylenol 10 MG/ML with miconazole nitrate 20 MG/ML and salicylic acid 20 MG/ML, as well as stand‑alone medicated shampoos containing miconazole, urea 100 MG/ML, or zinc pyrithione 19 MG/ML. The dose form “chlorhexidine / ketoconazole / salicyloyl phytosphingosine Medicated Shampoo” links the product class to this specific triple‑active combination. Collectively, these relationships illustrate the extensive use of chlorhexidine as a central antiseptic component across diverse medicated shampoo formulations designed to provide targeted antimicrobial, antifungal, keratolytic, and soothing effects for scalp and dermatologic therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 2 -- Community 887
retrieved_docs.content: Chlorhexidin (chlorhexidine) serves as the primary active moiety in a wide range of medicated shampoos, each formulated with specific concentrations and additional therapeutic agents. Products that contain chlorhexidine as the sole active ingredient include chlorhexidine gluconate 10 MG/ML Medicated Shampoo, chlorhexidine gluconate 20 MG/ML Medicated Shampoo, chlorhexidine gluconate 40 MG/ML Medicated Shampoo, and chlorhexidine Medicated Shampoo. Higher‑strength formulations such as chlorhexidine gluconate 40 MG/ML Medicated Shampoo [Vital‑Chlor] also rely on chlorhexidine as the key antiseptic component. Several shampoos combine chlorhexidine with antifungal or keratolytic agents to broaden their clinical utility. Chlorhexidine gluconate 20 MG/ML / ketoconazole 10 MG/ML Medicated Shampoo [Keto‑C] and chlorhexidine gluconate 23 MG/ML / ketoconazole 10 MG/ML Medicated Shampoo [Ketochlor] pair chlorhexidine with ketoconazole to target fungal scalp infections. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 1 -- Community 48
retrieved_docs.content: Ketoconazole is a widely used antifungal agent incorporated in numerous medicated shampoos (e.g., Nizoral 10 mg/mL and 20 mg/mL, Keto‑C, Vital‑Ketodine, Ketochlor, Pharmaseb, and generic ketoconazole shampoo). In many formulations it is combined with the broad‑spectrum antiseptic chlorhexidine gluconate (10–40 mg/mL), the topical corticosteroid hydrocortisone (5 mg/mL, 10 mg/mL, or hydrocortisone acetate 5 mg/mL), the keratolytic/skin‑conditioning agent salicyloyl phytosphingosine (0.5 mg/mL), chloroxylenol, or other antifungals such as miconazole nitrate (20 mg/mL) and climbazole (5 mg/mL). Representative combination products include chlorhexidine 20 mg/mL / hydrocortisone 10 mg/mL / ketoconazole 10 mg/mL (Keto‑C, Vital‑Ketodine), chlorhexidine 20 mg/mL / ketoconazole 10 mg/mL (Ketochlor), chlorhexidine 20 mg/mL / miconazole nitrate 20 mg/mL (Malaseb, MiconaHex+Triz), chlorhexidine 30 mg/mL / climbazole 5 mg/mL / salicyloyl phytosphingosine 0.5 mg/mL, chlorhexidine 40 mg/mL (with or without salicyloyl phytosphingosine), chloroxylenol / ketoconazole, chloroxylenol / miconazole nitrate / salicylic acid, and hydrocortisone‑only shampoos (5 mg/mL or acetate 5 mg/mL). Zinc pyrithione 19 mg/mL is also marketed as an antibacterial/anti‑seborrheic medicated shampoo. The “medicated shampoo” dosage form is a topical scalp preparation intended for fungal, bacterial, and seborrheic scalp disorders, delivering antiseptic, antifungal, anti‑inflammatory, and keratolytic actions. The extensive range of active ingredients and concentrations allows clinicians to tailor therapy to the specific microbial, fungal, inflammatory, or keratolytic needs of individual patients. From a safety standpoint, topical ketoconazole‑containing shampoos are contraindicated in patients receiving the systemic agents dihydroergotamine mesylate, dihydroergotamine tartrate, or ubrogepant due to potential adverse drug interactions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1465
retrieved_docs.content: Antiinfectives and antiseptics for local oral treatment are classified as members of the peroxid vodíku (hydrogen peroxide) group, indicating that these oral antimicrobial agents are formulated on the basis of hydrogen peroxide and are used for topical infection control within the mouth. This relationship underscores hydrogen peroxide’s role as the active component in such local oral antiinfective and antiseptic preparations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 2 -- Community 462
retrieved_docs.content: Antibiotic throat preparations are a specific category of throat preparations that are formulated to deliver antimicrobial agents directly to the oropharyngeal region. Within this class, fusafungin and gramicidin are recognized as constituent members, providing the antibacterial activity characteristic of these preparations. By being classified as throat preparations, antibiotic throat preparations are intended for topical use in the mouth and throat to treat or prevent bacterial infections, leveraging the therapeutic properties of fusafungin and gramicidin to achieve effective local antimicrobial effects.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1469
retrieved_docs.content: Tetracycline and its derivatives formulated for topical application are classified as antibiotics for topical use.
